message.content:
The salt spray had long since bleached Elias’s skin to the color of old parchment, and the rhythm of the waves had become the only clock he trusted. For twenty years, he had tended the Needle, a jagged spire of granite jutting out from the edge of the Blackwood Coast. He was a man of silence, content to let the gulls scream and the wind howl while he polished the great Fresnel lens until it caught the moonlight like a diamond.

It was a Tuesday, or perhaps a Wednesday—the days blurred into a gray smear of fog and rain—when the bottle washed up. It wasn’t the pristine, glass-blown vessel of romantic fiction. It was a jagged shard of a green beer bottle, sealed with wax that had turned brittle and brown. It had been battered against the rocks, tumbled by the tide, and spat out onto the pebbles at the base of the lighthouse steps.

Elias picked it up. The glass was cold, slick with algae. He hesitated, his calloused thumb tracing the rough edge of the cork. He hadn’t opened mail in months. He hadn’t spoken to another human being in weeks. But something about the bottle’s desperation, its sheer improbability, tugged at a dormant part of his chest.

He carried it inside, the heavy oak door groaning behind him. The lantern room was warm, smelling of oil and brass. He sat at his small wooden table, the beam of the lighthouse sweeping across the walls in a hypnotic arc. With a pocket knife, he pried at the wax. It cracked with a sound like a breaking bone.

He reached in and pulled out a tightly rolled scrap of paper. It was thin, almost translucent, written in a hand so fine it looked like spider silk.

Elias unfolded it with trembling fingers. The ink
